title.title: What's Next for Blockchain?
title.subtitle: Applications, ecosystem, evolution of the web, and the future of decentralized technology

ethereum.recapTail: . Vitalik's response: don't patch Bitcoin — build a new chain with a real virtual machine.
ethereum.heading: Ethereum — The Programmable Blockchain
ethereum.lead: Bitcoin proved decentralised money was possible. Ethereum asked: what if you could run any program on a blockchain?
ethereum.vitalikBio: Born 1994 in Russia, raised in Canada. Published the Ethereum whitepaper in late 2013 — aged
ethereum.whyP1A: Vitalik was an early Bitcoin contributor and journalist. He saw that Bitcoin's scripting language was
ethereum.whyP1Tail: — great for currency, useless for building applications. Several projects had tried to layer features on top of Bitcoin (Colored Coins, Mastercoin) but all hit the same ceiling.
ethereum.whyP2A: His proposal: a new blockchain with a
ethereum.whyP2Mid: (the EVM) that could execute any arbitrary program — called a
ethereum.whyP2Tail: . Developers would finally be able to build decentralised applications without a trusted server.

events.label: Crowdsale raises $18M — largest at the time
The DAO hack — first major test of governance
The Merge — switches from PoW to Proof of Stake
Pectra upgrade — smart accounts, higher validator limits, blob scaling
Fusaka upgrade — full PeerDAS, EOF, and deeper L2 scaling

ethereum.rankNote: Largest blockchain by market cap and developer activity

ethAccounts.recapA: Remember — a wallet holds keys, not coins. Ethereum runs the same idea, but with
ethAccounts.recapTail: of account: one human-controlled, one code-controlled.
ethAccounts.heading: Ethereum: Accounts & Gas
ethAccounts.lead: Two concepts you must understand before touching any Ethereum application. Everything else is built on top of these.

eoaPoints.tail: — whoever holds the key owns the account
— send ETH, deploy contracts, call functions

eoaPoints.leadA: Has a balance in ETH. No code stored.
Examples: your MetaMask wallet, a hardware wallet, an exchange hot wallet

contractPoints.tail: — no private key, no human owner
transactions — only executes when called by an EOA or another contract

contractPoints.leadA: Has a balance in ETH + stores code and state permanently on-chain
Examples: Uniswap DEX, USDC token, an NFT collection

ethAccounts.keyRuleBody: Every transaction on Ethereum originates from an EOA. Smart contracts cannot act spontaneously — they need a human (or a keeper bot) to trigger them.
ethAccounts.gasHeader: Gas — The Fuel of the EVM
ethAccounts.gasIntroTail: — a unit that measures computational work. Gas prevents infinite loops and spam: you must pay for every instruction executed by the EVM.

gasRows.note: View call — never hits the chain
Cheap — stored in logs, not state
Base cost for every ETH transfer
Pays for every byte of bytecode

gasFormulaPoints.tail: Set by the protocol, burned (EIP-1559, since 2021)
You pay the validator to include your transaction faster
Gas price is denominated in gwei (1 gwei = 0.000000001 ETH)
If a transaction runs out of gas mid-execution — it reverts, but you still pay for the work done

ethApps.recapBody: You've seen gas pay for computation. Time to look at what people actually build with it — every example below is a smart contract holding ETH and following rules.
ethApps.heading: What Ethereum Enables: DeFi, NFTs, DAOs
ethApps.lead: Three categories of applications that did not exist before programmable blockchains — each replacing or disrupting a traditional institution.

apps.title: DeFi
apps.subtitle: Decentralised Finance
apps.description: Financial services — lending, borrowing, trading, earning yield — running entirely in smart contracts. No KYC, no opening hours, no intermediary taking a cut.

examples.name: Uniswap
examples.desc: Decentralised exchange — swap any ERC-20 token via automated market maker (AMM)

examples.name: Aave
examples.desc: Lend and borrow crypto — interest rates set algorithmically by supply/demand

examples.name: MakerDAO / DAI
examples.desc: Algorithmic stablecoin — mint DAI by locking ETH as collateral in a smart contract

apps.risk: Smart contract bugs, oracle manipulation, liquidation cascades
apps.stat: $40B+ Total Value Locked in DeFi protocols

apps.title: NFTs
apps.subtitle: Non-Fungible Tokens
apps.description: Provable, transferable digital ownership on-chain. Each NFT is a unique token (ERC-721) whose ownership history is permanently recorded. No one can deny ownership — the blockchain is the title deed.

examples.name: ENS (.eth domains)
examples.desc: Blockchain-native usernames — pay once, own forever. No renewal extortion.

examples.name: Proof of attendance (POAPs)
examples.desc: On-chain badges proving you attended events — cryptographic credentials

examples.name: Real estate deeds
examples.desc: Propy sold the first NFT property — immutable ownership transfer on-chain

apps.risk: Metadata stored off-chain (IPFS) can disappear — "you don't own the image, you own the receipt"
apps.stat: $41B traded in NFTs in 2021 — peak. Now utility-focused.

apps.title: DAOs
apps.subtitle: Decentralised Autonomous Organisations
apps.description: An organisation governed by smart contracts and token votes instead of a board of directors. Anyone holding governance tokens can propose and vote on decisions — the outcome executes automatically on-chain.

examples.name: MakerDAO
examples.desc: MKR holders vote on DAI stability parameters — billions in protocol policy decided on-chain

examples.name: Uniswap DAO
examples.desc: UNI holders control the $6B+ Uniswap treasury — voted to deploy to new chains

examples.name: Gitcoin DAO
examples.desc: Funds open-source development via quadratic voting — community decides who gets grants

apps.risk: Voter apathy, whale capture, slow governance for emergencies

useCases.recapBody: Remember the village ledger — every trust problem you saw there shows up again, at industry scale.
useCases.heading: Blockchain Use Cases — Three Patterns, Twelve Industries
useCases.leadA: Zoom out from DeFi · NFTs · DAOs. The same
useCases.leadTail: show up across every sector — wherever there are middlemen, paper trails, or coordination headaches.

groups.tagline: Money, claims, and contracts settle directly between parties — banks, brokers, and clearing houses become optional.

industries.name: Finance & DeFi
industries.hook: Lending, trading, payments — no bank, open 24/7

industries.name: Capital Markets
industries.hook: Tokenised securities settle in seconds, not T+2 days

industries.name: Insurance
industries.hook: Parametric policies auto-pay on verified events

industries.name: CBDCs
industries.hook: Programmable state money — power and peril (see Ethics)

groups.tagline: Anything that needs an unbroken chain of custody gets one — built in, not bolted on.

industries.name: Supply Chain
industries.hook: Track & trace from raw material to consumer

industries.name: Global Trade
industries.hook: Digital letters of credit replace 5-day paper trails

industries.name: Media & Entertainment
industries.hook: Royalties to collaborators on every play, automatically

groups.tagline: People and organisations coordinate without a single platform or registrar in the middle.

industries.name: Digital Identity
industries.hook: You own your credentials, prove age without revealing birthdate

industries.name: Government
industries.hook: Verifiable voting, immutable land registry, transparent procurement

industries.name: Energy & Sustainability
industries.hook: Carbon credits without double-counting, P2P energy trading

industries.name: Law & Real Estate
industries.hook: Escrow + title transfers auto-executed, fractional ownership

useCases.noticeBodyTail: ) shows up across supply chain, healthcare, and media. Industries are different surfaces; the underlying primitive is the same — which is why a Course-2 platform choice can serve multiple Course-3 business cases.

ecosystem.recapBody: Each use case you just saw lives somewhere in this stack — same nodes from §1 at the bottom, same contracts as DeFi/NFTs in the middle, you at the top.
ecosystem.heading: The Blockchain & Web3 Ecosystem
ecosystem.lead: A layered stack — from raw infrastructure at the bottom to end users at the top.

web3.kickerBodyA: Quick historical context: how the web's
web3.kickerTail: has shifted in 30 years — and why Web3 looks different from what came before.
web3.heading: The Evolution of the Internet
web3.lead: Each era defined by what users could do — and who owned the data.

web1.label: Web 1.0
web1.quote: "The internet was a library. You could only look."

web2.label: Web 2.0
bullets: Platforms own and monetize your data
Free services — you are the product
web2.quote: "The internet became interactive. But platforms kept the keys."

web3.label: Web 3.0
web3.quote: "The internet becomes owned by its users. No platform can take it away."

items.option1: Centralized servers controlled by a company
items.option2: Smart contracts on a blockchain + decentralized storage

items.option1: Company owns and controls user data
items.option2: Users own their data and assets
No single point of failure — runs as long as the network exists

items.option1: Company can ban users, remove content, change terms
items.option2: No entity can censor transactions once on-chain
Token incentives, protocol fees, community governance

items.option1: Spotify — company controls catalog, pays artists pennies
items.option2: Audius — artists upload directly, fans pay via $AUDIO tokens

ethics.recapTail: that protects the chain from tampering also protects bad rules from being undone. Same property, two faces.
ethics.heading: Ethical Considerations
ethics.lead: Blockchain is not inherently good or bad — its impact depends on how it is designed, governed, and used.

themes.title: ⚡ Energy
themes.desc: Bitcoin PoW uses as much power as some countries. Ethereum's PoS cut its footprint by 99.95%.

themes.title: 👁️ Surveillance vs Privacy
themes.desc: Public chains are transparent forever — accountability and privacy are in direct tension.

themes.title: 🔒 Irreversibility
themes.desc: "Code is law" has limits. Transactions can't be reversed, even when real people are harmed.

themes.title: 🌍 Access & Inclusion
themes.desc: 1.4 billion unbanked adults could access financial services with just a smartphone and a wallet.

good.title: GiveDirectly — Transparent Humanitarian Aid
points: NGO operating in Kenya and Uganda — sends cash directly to people in extreme poverty via blockchain wallets
Zero intermediaries — every transfer is traceable on-chain; donors can verify their money arrived
In traditional aid, up to 30% is lost to administration, NGO salaries, and corruption — blockchain cuts this to near zero
Recipients spend freely — no government or NGO can dictate what the money is used for
Smart contracts automate disbursements — no bank account required, just a mobile wallet

good.title: Proof of Humanity — Identity Without Institutions
points: Ethereum-based registry: prove you are a real human via a short video + vouching by existing members
No government, bank, or company controls the list — no one can delete you
Foundation for UBI experiments: verified humans receive a monthly UBI token airdrop automatically
Enables bot-free social networks and one-person-one-vote governance on-chain
Used by Kleros (decentralised court) and other dApps as a sybil-resistance layer

control.title: Digital Yuan (e-CNY) — Programmable Money, Programmable Control
points: Expiry dates — government stimulus yuan must be spent within a deadline or it disappears
Geographic restrictions — funds can be locked to specific regions or districts
Category bans — purchases like alcohol, gambling, or political donations can be auto-blocked
Instant freezing — any wallet disabled in seconds, no court order needed
Full real-time surveillance — every transaction visible to the state by design

control.title: Digital Euro — Coming Soon, Questions Remain
points: ECB plans to launch a CBDC — marketed as a convenient, modern form of cash
Proposed €3,000 holding cap prevents using it as savings — steering behaviour by design
Unlike physical cash — anonymous and unconditional — every CBDC transaction is permanently logged
Infrastructure built for "features" today can silently add restrictions tomorrow
Civil society groups and MEPs have raised formal concerns about financial sovereignty

future.title: Future Trends
future.description: The next decade of blockchain will be shaped by convergence with other technologies and solutions to current limitations.

trends.title: 🤖 AI + Blockchain
trends.desc: Verifiable AI outputs, decentralized training data, provenance tracking for AI-generated content. Blockchain can prove what is real.

trends.title: 🔮 Zero-Knowledge Proofs
trends.desc: Prove something is true without revealing the underlying data. Enables privacy-preserving identity, voting, and transactions at scale.

trends.title: 🔗 Layer 0 & Interoperability
trends.desc: Protocols like Polkadot and Cosmos connect different blockchains. The future is a network of networks, not one chain to rule them all.

trends.title: 🪪 Decentralized Identity (DID)
trends.desc: Self-sovereign identity lets you prove credentials without sharing personal data. One identity across all services, owned by you.

trends.title: 🧾 Account Abstraction
trends.desc: Makes crypto wallets work like normal accounts: social recovery, gas sponsoring, session keys. The UX revolution blockchain needs.

trends.title: 🏗️ Real-World Assets (RWAs)
trends.desc: Tokenizing real estate, bonds, commodities on-chain. BlackRock, Franklin Templeton, and others are already doing this at scale.

keyPoints: AI + blockchain solves the provenance and trust problem for digital content
ZK proofs unlock privacy without sacrificing verifiability
Interoperability protocols will connect isolated blockchain ecosystems
Account abstraction will make crypto accessible to mainstream users

discussion.prompt: What industry do you think could benefit most from blockchain technology in the next 10 years?
guidingQuestions: What problems exist in that industry that blockchain could solve?
What are the potential challenges to adoption?
How would blockchain improve transparency or efficiency?
What stakeholders might resist this change and why?

quizWeb3.question: What is the fundamental shift that Web3 represents compared to Web2?
options: Faster internet speeds and better streaming quality
Users own their data, identity, and digital assets instead of platforms
Free access to all premium content and services
Government control and regulation of all internet content
quizWeb3.explanation: Web3 shifts ownership from platforms to users through decentralization and cryptographic keys. Instead of companies owning your data, you control your identity and assets via wallets and on-chain protocols.

quizDapp.question: What is a key advantage of a dApp over a traditional centralized application?
options: dApps are always faster and cheaper to use
dApps have no single point of failure — they run as long as the blockchain network exists
dApps don't require an internet connection to function
dApps are always free and generate no revenue
quizDapp.explanation: Because dApps run on decentralized blockchain networks via smart contracts, they have no central server that can fail or be shut down. As long as the underlying blockchain network operates, the dApp continues to function.

quizLayers.question: Which layer of the Web3 ecosystem contains protocols like Ethereum, Solana, and Polygon?
quizLayers.explanation: Ethereum, Solana, and Polygon are L1 blockchains or L2 scaling solutions. They sit between raw infrastructure (nodes, validators, P2P network) and the application protocols (DeFi, NFTs, DAOs) built on top of them.

takeaways.title: Section 3 — Key Takeaways
items: Blockchain use cases span 8+ industries: finance, healthcare, supply chain, voting, identity, real estate, sustainability, and digital art
The Web3 ecosystem is a layered stack — from nodes and validators at the bottom to wallets, dApps, and users at the top
Web3 = Read + Write + Own: the key shift is that users own their data and digital assets instead of platforms
dApps replace centralized servers with smart contracts — removing single points of failure and platform control
Ethical trade-offs are real: energy consumption, privacy vs transparency, irreversibility, and dual-use potential
Future trends — ZK proofs, AI integration, DID, account abstraction — will define blockchain's next decade

knowledgeWall.heading: Knowledge Wall
knowledgeWall.leadA: Every term you've met across Sections 1, 2, and 3 — in one place. Hover any brick to recall its meaning.
knowledgeWall.hoverHint: Hover or tap a brick below to read its definition.

bricks.defn: Distributed Ledger Technology — a database replicated across many independent computers, with no central authority.
A one-way function that turns any input into a fixed-size fingerprint. You can't reverse it; tiny input changes flip the output completely.
The specific hash function Bitcoin uses. Always produces 64 hex characters.
Changing one bit of input completely changes the hash output. The "spread" property that makes hashing useful.
Once written into the chain, a record can't be silently altered without leaving evidence everywhere downstream.
Stronger than "tamper-proof": any change breaks visible cryptographic links, so the tampering can't hide.
Replacing trust in people or institutions with trust in math, code, and incentives.
A container of transactions with a header that cryptographically links to the previous block.
The sequence of blocks, each tied to its predecessor through a hash pointer.
The very first block. Has no predecessor — its prev-hash field is conventionally all zeros.
A block's metadata: version, prev hash, merkle root, timestamp, bits (difficulty), and nonce.
A binary tree of hashes. Pairs are hashed together repeatedly until one root remains.
A single hash that summarises every transaction in a block. Change any tx → root changes.
A number a miner cycles through until the block's hash meets the difficulty target.
How many leading zeros the hash must have. Adjusts every 2,016 blocks to keep ~10 min block time.
Approximate time the miner stamped the block. Lives inside the header.
Software that holds your keys. It does NOT hold coins — coins live on-chain. The keys prove the coins are yours.
The one secret that authorises spending. Like a PIN, like a house key. Lose it = lose the funds.
Derived from the private key one-way. Used by others to verify your signatures. Safe to share.
A short hash of the public key. Like an IBAN or a mailbox slot — anyone can send to it.
12 or 24 ordinary English words encoding the master secret. Regenerates the entire wallet on any device.
Cryptographic proof a message was approved by the holder of a specific private key, without revealing the key.
A computer running the blockchain software, storing the chain and validating new transactions/blocks.
Peer-to-peer. No central server — every node talks directly to other nodes.
How thousands of independent nodes agree on the same state of the chain.
Miners spend electricity guessing nonces. Whoever finds a valid hash first wins the right to add a block.
Validators stake tokens as collateral. Misbehave and the stake is slashed. Ethereum switched to PoS in 2022.
Running computers to solve PoW puzzles in exchange for newly minted coins + transaction fees.
If one entity controls majority hash power, they can rewrite recent history. The reason chains want many nodes.
The classic problem: how to reach agreement across a network where some participants may lie or fail.
A split in the chain. Soft fork = backward-compatible; hard fork = creates two incompatible chains.
Trying to send the same coin twice. Consensus + the longest-chain rule prevents it.
The pseudonymous creator of Bitcoin. Published the whitepaper Oct 2008, mined the genesis block Jan 2009, vanished 2010.
Bitcoin's 9-page founding document. Surprisingly readable. Linked in the Bibliography.
The absolute maximum number of bitcoins that will ever exist. Hard-coded by every full node.
Every ~4 years the block reward halves. Schedules supply decay; last halving expected around 2140.
Unspent Transaction Output. Bitcoin tracks "unspent coin chunks", not account balances.
Bitcoin's intentionally limited scripting language. Not Turing-complete — no loops, no persistent state.
A Layer-2 network on top of Bitcoin for fast, cheap micropayments. Settles back to L1 periodically.
Simplified Payment Verification. Light clients use Merkle proofs to verify a tx is in a block without storing the chain.
The first programmable blockchain. Launched 2015 by Vitalik Buterin. Where smart contracts became practical.
Ethereum Virtual Machine. The global computer that executes smart contracts identically on every node.
Code stored on a blockchain that runs deterministically when called. The data IS the code IS the rules.
The unit measuring computational work on the EVM. Every operation costs gas; you pay for what you use.
1 gwei = 0.000 000 001 ETH. The unit gas prices are quoted in.
Externally Owned Account. An account controlled by a private key — your MetaMask wallet is one.
An account controlled by code, not a key. Holds a smart contract's state. Can't initiate transactions on its own.
The 2021 fee market upgrade. Base fee is burned, optional priority tip goes to the validator.
Sept 2022 — Ethereum switched from PoW to PoS. Cut its energy use by 99.95%.
Decentralised application — a frontend talking to smart contracts on-chain instead of a company server.
Decentralised Finance. Lending, trading, derivatives running entirely in smart contracts.
Non-Fungible Token. A unique on-chain asset (ERC-721) with a permanent ownership history.
Decentralised Autonomous Organisation. Governance by smart contract + token votes instead of a board.
The Ethereum standard for fungible tokens (every USDC, every DAI, every LINK).
The Ethereum standard for non-fungible tokens (NFTs).
A token pegged to a stable asset, usually the US dollar. USDC, USDT, DAI.
A service that brings off-chain data on-chain (prices, sports scores, weather). Chainlink is the leading provider.
InterPlanetary File System. Content-addressed decentralised storage often used to host NFT metadata.
A base-layer blockchain (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana). Where consensus + final settlement happens.
A network built on top of an L1 to scale throughput while inheriting its security. Arbitrum, Optimism, Base.
An L2 that batches transactions off-chain and posts a compressed proof back to L1.
Zero-Knowledge Proof. Prove a statement is true without revealing the underlying data.
Wallets that behave like smart contracts: social recovery, gasless txs, session keys. UX upgrade.
Real-World Assets. Tokenising real estate, bonds, commodities on-chain. BlackRock, Franklin Templeton are early.
Central Bank Digital Currency. State-issued digital money — programmable, traceable, sometimes restrictable.
Decentralised Identity. User-controlled digital identity, no central registry to revoke you.

teamCheckpoint.title: Form your team
teamCheckpoint.subtitle: The team project runs across all five courses. Find your teammates now — you'll start using the team in Day 2 and Day 3.

doNow.leadA: Exchange contact details — WhatsApp / Telegram / email — so you can coordinate between sessions.
Pick a working name for your team and a primary point of contact.

discuss.tail: you find interesting or work in.

discuss.leadA: Note any business pain points you've personally experienced — paper-heavy processes, slow settlements, trust gaps, etc.
Sketch a few candidate problems freely. You can refine — or completely change direction — as you learn more across Days 2 and 3.

bring.leadA: Your team's contact list — everyone in one chat group.
A short list (~3) of pain points worth exploring.
Curiosity about which blockchain platforms could fit them.

teamCheckpoint.footnoteBody: Day 2 (Blockchain Platforms) will introduce platform trade-offs you'll need to choose between. Day 3 (Smart Contracts) is where your team's idea becomes a concrete design.
